title: Flanger
summary: Creates a whooshing, comb-filtering effect by mixing the signal with a slightly delayed version of itself. Best used sparingly in breakdown sections and transitions — not as a constant effect.
steps: Add Flanger to a pad or synth — works best on textural or atmospheric sounds
Rate: how fast the effect sweeps. 0.1–0.5Hz = slow, evolving sweep (good for breakdowns). Sync to tempo for rhythmic flanging
Feedback: how pronounced the metallic comb-filter sound is. Low feedback = subtle movement. High feedback = obvious, metallic character
Dry/Wet: 20–40% for subtle movement. 60%+ for obvious flange effect
Hi-Pass and Lo-Pass controls: filter the effect so it only applies to certain frequencies — use Hi-Pass 200Hz to keep the flanger from affecting the low end
For a breakdown sweep: automate the Rate or Feedback to increase over 4–8 bars as the breakdown builds
tips: Flanger is most powerful when used sparingly — one moment in a breakdown, not throughout a track. Constant flanging becomes tiring quickly
Automation is key: a flanger that slowly increases its Rate over 8 bars creates a sense of building tension that static settings can't achieve

The comb-filtering effect interacts with the harmonic content of the sound — try on different synth patches and compare. Sounds with many harmonics (Reese, pads with lots of overtones) flange more dramatically
Flanger + Reverb on a breakdown pad: the flange adds movement, the reverb adds space. Together they create the 'dissolving' breakdown sound
practice_hint: In any spinoff with a breakdown section, add Flanger to the chord pad during the breakdown. Set Rate 0.2Hz, Feedback 30%, Dry/Wet 30%. Listen to how it changes the texture of the breakdown.
ableton_version_note: Available in all editions of Live 9+.
